abstract:
To access architecturally complex natural products, synthetic chemists typically design bespoke strategies tailored to the construction of a single target skeleton. In contrast, biosynthetic assembly lines often employ a more efficient logic, utilizing divergent intramolecular cyclizations of polyunsaturated common intermediates to generate diverse arrays of molecular scaffolds. Inspired by these biogenetic principles, we report the development of an artificial divergent assembly line capable of generating an unprecedented variety of terpenoid indole alkaloid frameworks. 

Our approach facilitates practical access to multipotent intermediates, enabling the systematic diversification of skeletal, stereochemical, and functional properties without necessitating the structural simplification often seen in synthetic analogs. By leveraging three distinct modes of [4+2] cyclization and two types of redox-mediated annulations, we achieved divergent access to five skeletally unique frameworks—including iboga-, aspidosperma-, andranginine-, and ngouniensine-type skeletons, as well as a non-natural variant—in only 6 to 9 steps from tryptamine. The utility of this platform is further demonstrated by the total syntheses of (±)-vincadifformine, (±)-andranginine, and (−)-catharanthine. Because natural products possess rigid, sp3-rich architectures that enable specific molecular recognition through multi-point interactions, this synthetic strategy provides a robust foundation for creating high-quality screening libraries for drug discovery.
